title: The Macro Minute: Is the disinflation process still intact?

Darius Del to present our macro minute
for Wednesday February 12th 2025 as
always we'll start with the executive
summary from today's lead off morning
note if you would like to review the
analysis supporting these conclusions as
well as what specific changes you need
to make in your portfolio to remain on
the right side of Market risk need to be
a client of 42 macro so today's key
macro question is is the disinflation
process still intact our answers are yes
for now but if with emphasis on for now
because we have a highly non-consensus
view that core BCE inflation will bottom
in q1 and begin a multi- quarter uptrend
in Q2 both the fed and Wall Street
Economist consensus currently have core
PC persistently decelerating throughout
2025 our hawkish inflation Outlook was
corroborated by the January CPI data
which were admittedly and likely
flattered by postco seasonal adjustment
discrepancies our secular inflation
model AG a aims to gauge the impact of
structural changes in the US economy
upon the equilibrium rate of core PC
inflation
since the Inception of the model when we
introduced our sticky inflation theme in
January of 2022 it is signaled that the
core PC the equilibrium rate of core PC
inflation is in the high twos low threes
our Grim model is calling for core PC
inflation the bottom of the mid twos
before cycling higher like cyclical
processes always do that is consistent
with our industry-leading Deep dive
empirical study on the US business cycle
dynamics that confirms there is zero
evidence of inflation breaking down
durably below Trend without a recession
that same model continues to assign a
low probability to a developing
recession in the US economy all told
investors should expect better behaved
inflation data for the next few months
by the summer however we believe the
consensus narratives regarding the
disinflation process and the degree to
which monetary policy is restrictive
will change while rate hikes are
unlikely over the medium term a dramatic
hawkish provision to the fed's estimate
of neutral is an underpriced risk for
the bond market and by extension risk
asset valuations in the second half of
2025 transitioning to our 42 micro
dashboard is always a wrap up the
question from our community says
thoughts on the reverse uo hitting the
lowest level in almost five years so a
few thoughts one this was very
deliberate I recall that uh secretary
Yellen uh to treasury secretary Jenny
Yellen uh was concentrating issuance on
the short end of the treasury curve to
satisfy excess demand in the marketplace
and among commercial banks for shorter
duration uh treasury Securities and so
so um that allowed a lot of that excess
liquidity which had fleed duration risk
in the bond market and got wound up on
the feds reverse supero facility uh uh
that that all that excess liquidity was
able to kind of get freed I mean we've
we basically down about $2.4 trillion
dollar from the height of the RP I want
to say in late 2022 or early 2023 so uh
one it was deliberate two uh what it is
is an indication is that the excess
liquidity uh uh in the US Financial
system is getting to a level where it's
no longer likely to be excess liquidity
now there's two layers of excess
liquidity one there the excess liquidity
that we can see show up on the feds
reverse repo facility that does not have
a home in shorter duration instruments
either in the repo Market or in the bill
Market but there's also the excess
liquidity in terms of the spread between
adequate reserves and ample reserves uh
the FED has been very uh reticent to uh
Define exactly what that means in in
nominal terms or ratio terms so we as
investors have to use a myriad of
analysis analyses to kind of figure out
and gauge that uh and so right now the
FED still thinks it's in ample reserves
regime it's trying to go to U sorry no
if think is in a um uh yeah they want to
say ample reserves regime wants to get
to an adequate reserves regime uh uh and
ultimately uh our ability to fill that
out as investors we you know will be a
function of us being able to spot um
changes in repo market dynamics pricing
in the repo Market you know typically
when you see spreads right now the
spreads between you know uh you know
General collateral repo for instance and
and thetive fed runs rate is negative
like the FED funds rate is higher than
the the spreads you're you're seeing in
the repo Market which ultimately means
that there is excess liquidity in the
repo Market because otherwise you would
be able to
see that spread be positive and so you
know we're tracking things like that
we're tracking the RRP to to figure out
okay when are we going to get to a quote
unquote ample level of reserves that
causes the FED to stop quantitative
tightening it's our expect ation
alongside the expectation of primary
dealers and the treasury borrowing
advisory committee that the FED stops qt
or or balance sheet runoff they're not
doing QT uh they'll stop the treasury
portion of balance sheet runoff uh in
the summer of this year and likely
continue on with the um the mortgage
back security portion uh beyond that
because they they they have already
stated their intention to get NBS off
their portfolio so it's going to be a
while before they do that given the lack
of prepayments in the mortgage Market
but that's a totally separate
conversation so uh in summary we're
running out of excess liquidity uh
liquidity is still relatively uh ample
just judged by the fact that the FED
still believes uh liquidity is ample we
can observe this through other metrics
in the repo Market as well but
ultimately we're going to be getting to
a place where the FED needs to stop
balance sheet runoff in the coming
quarters our expectation on that is in
the summer so hope everyone U enjoyed
that we'll catch you back here uh
